text.title: Secret Consumer Discovery Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules that govern enumeration of every consumer of a given secret across the stack. Any missed consumer = production outage at rotation time.

rule.statement: Before any search, MUST build the alias set: logical name, every known env-var spelling, every file-path form, every KMS/store path, every short ID. Searches with fewer than 3 aliases for a secret older than 12 months are REJECTED — old secrets always have alias drift.
rule.rationale: A single-string search misses 30-60% of consumers because Devs spell secrets inconsistently across repos. Alias-set-first is the only way to avoid blind spots.

rule.statement: Discovery MUST query ALL SIX consumer surfaces: (1) source code in every repo, (2) CI/CD pipeline definitions, (3) secret-store audit log (last 90 days reads), (4) scheduler/cron jobs, (5) runtime configs (k8s ConfigMaps, .env mounts, systemd EnvironmentFiles), (6) infrastructure-as-code (Terraform/Pulumi). Skipping any source = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Most rotation breakage comes from sources teams forget — cron jobs and IaC pipelines especially. The six-source sweep is the minimum honest discovery.

rule.statement: The secret-store audit log (e.g. Vault audit, AWS CloudTrail GetSecretValue) is the GROUND TRUTH for active consumers. Any consumer that hit the store in the last 30 days MUST appear in the registry. Code-grep matches without audit-log hits are tagged `unverified` and surface for follow-up.
rule.rationale: Code may reference a secret that the runtime never actually fetches. Audit log proves who is really live; code search proves who is theoretically a consumer. Both are needed; audit log is authoritative.

rule.statement: Output MUST be a structured registry: `consumers: [{name, type: code|ci|cron|runtime|iac, location: <path>, owner: <handle>, last_fetch: <iso8601 or null>, rotation_blast_radius: low|medium|high}]`. Free-form text registry = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Untyped registries cannot be programmatically consumed by the rotation runbook. The typed form is the contract between discovery and rotation.

rule.statement: Every consumer MUST carry a `rotation_blast_radius` rating with a rationale string. Ratings: low = restart consumer to pick up new value, medium = cascading dep needs coordinated restart, high = consumer is a database / payment processor / external integration with no graceful reload. Unrated consumers = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Without blast-radius ratings the rotation operator can't sequence the cutover. SOC2/ISO auditors also want documented risk per consumer.

rule.statement: The registry MUST be versioned (git or wiki history) and signed by a named human reviewer before the rotation runbook is allowed to start. Registry older than 30 days at runbook start = MUST refresh.
rule.rationale: Staleness is the most common failure mode. A 6-month-old registry is worse than no registry because operators trust it.

example.title: Registry excerpt
example.text: secret: STRIPE_API_KEY aliases: [STRIPE_KEY, stripe_sk, sk-stripe] consumers: [{name: payment-api, type: runtime, location: k8s/prod/payment-api/env, owner: @ops, last_fetch: 2026-05-19T11:02Z, rotation_blast_radius: high}, {name: nightly-recon, type: cron, location: hosts/recon-01/crontab, owner: @finance, last_fetch: 2026-05-20T03:00Z, rotation_blast_radius: medium}]

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
